text: CES 2026 felt like the moment robotics finally crossed the line between futuristic demos and realworld deployment. Realics [music] showed how close machines are getting to real human emotion and expression. [music] Engine AI's T800 proved robots can take hits, fight back, [music] and stay standing. Turtle style robots showed robots can protect nature, too. And the new Atlas made it clear humanoid robots are officially [music] entering real factories. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome back to AI Nexus. Today we're breaking down what really happened at CES 2026 and why this year quietly changed the future of robots. Everywhere you turned in Las Vegas, machines weren't just posing for cameras. They were working, reacting, and even understanding. But among all of them, one robot stopped everyone in their tracks. At the robotics booth, one display instantly drew a crowd. A metallic gray, skull-faced robot perched motionless on a pedestal. Its square, angular head looked like a fusion of sculpture and circuitry with glowing eyes that tracked every movement nearby. It didn't speak or smile. It observed. This tabletop prototype [music] wasn't meant to charm. It was built to provoke, showing how emotion can be conveyed through motion alone. Subtle head turns and synchronized pupils hinted at mechanical consciousness beneath the surface. But that was just the prelude. Nearby stood David, real's newest male humanoid with magnetic [music] face panels, moving arms, and expressions that shift naturally midsentence. His eyes follow voices, his gestures flow in real time, and his reactions feel startlingly human. Returning beside him was Arya, the company's renowned female companion robot, now more polished, responsive, and expressive than ever. Together, they embodied Real Botics's mission to merge precision engineering with emotional realism. While realics focused on emotion and expression, Boston Dynamics took a very different route. Raw capability and industrial performance. Atlas, the world's most famous humanoid, made its first public stage appearance as an allect electric productionready model. The crowd gasped when the robot rose from a flat position using a joint flipping maneuver no human could replicate. Standing 6'2 with 56 degrees of freedom, Atlas waved to the audience, walked fluidly, and lifted heavy components as if gravity was optional. Its new electric actuators made every motion silent and smooth. Built in partnership with Google Deep Mind and Hyundai, this version can lift over 50 kg and operate 4 hours non-stop. It's not just a prototype anymore. 2026 marks the first year Atlas enters actual factories. Starting with Hyundai's robotics metap. And with Deep Mind's Gemini AI on board, Atlas can reason through complex tasks instead of just following code. The age of useful humanoids has officially begun. One of the most talked about machines at the show didn't focus on elegance or charm. It focused on dominance. The robot everyone's been calling the combat ready machine was Engine AI's T800. Before CES, many thought its viral fighting videos were CGI. At the show, the company proved everyone [music] wrong.

the most talked about machines at the show didn't focus on elegance or charm. It focused on dominance. The robot everyone's been calling the combat ready machine was Engine AI's T800. Before CES, many thought its viral fighting videos were CGI. At the show, the company proved everyone [music] wrong. demonstrated stability and balance under brute force. [music] Engineers pushed, kicked, even shoved it. Yet it barely wobbled. With 450 Newton meters of torque [music] and magnesium alloy limbs, this 5'8 powerhouse showed martial arts style kicks, running movements, and recovery precision that stunned the crowd. Powered by Nvidia Jetson, Thor delivering 2,000 tops of compute. The T800 wasn't built for show. It's built for function. And the price shocked everyone, too. Just $25,000. That's 1/4 the cost of most humanoids. Engine AI is betting big on affordability, turning cinematic robots into something universities and companies can actually own. If a robot like the T800 can take hits, [music] recover instantly, and stay balanced under force. Where do you think machines like this should never be used? Drop your thoughts below. This one's worth discussing. While some robots fought, others danced. Unitri Robotics returned with the G1, H2, and R1 models. Proof that mass production humanoids are real. The G1, compact at just over 4t [music] tall, threw punches and kicks in a choreographed boxing match against another G1. The crowd [music] roared as two machines sparred like professional fighters, complete with gloves and headgear. Then came the H2, the larger 6- foot tall unit capable of 360 Newton meters joint torque, flipping backward and striking sandbags with [music] stunning force. Even the smaller R1 showed agile movement for research labs. Unree isn't just showing robots, it's shipping them. The G1 is already available for purchase and the H2 begins delivery in April with prices starting near $13,500. [music] Untitree is redefining what mass market humanoid really means. But CES wasn't just about strength. It was also about companionship. Meanwhile, LG Electronics unveiled the show's most ambitious home robot, CLI. Standing on a wheeled base with twin arms, CLI showed what LG calls the zero labor home. It folded laundry, fetched [music] drinks, started the oven, and even located lost keys. It acted like a mobile household assistant, part butler, part AI coach. Powered by LG's new vision language model and VLA system, [music] CLID could see, understand, and act on voice commands in real time. Its [music] expressive head unit displayed emotions while connecting to the entire Thin Q ecosystem, so it could literally turn on your oven while you're still driving home. It's the boldest step yet toward a fully autonomous smart home, even if it's still just a demo. CES 2026 wasn't about concepts. It was about commitment. At CES, its A2 series danced, played music, and greeted

it's still just a demo. CES 2026 wasn't about concepts. It was about commitment. Subscribe if you want to track what happens next step by step. But here's what stunned attendees most. Agibbot arrived with 5,000 humanoids already deployed globally. At CES, its A2 series danced, played music, and greeted gracefully built, the A2 moved with convincing rhythm, [music] matching its expressive digital face. Agibbot also showcased Genie Sim 3.0, zero, a virtual training environment where its robots learn tasks from video data before performing them in the real world. With that system, [music] an Agibbot can master a new skill overnight. Already used in manufacturing, security, and education, Agibbot proved something profound. Humanoids are not the future. They're here. Right next to it, Germany's neurobotics showed 4NE1 generation 3 designed with Studio FA Porsche. It's sleek, powerful, and capable of lifting 100 kg thanks to high torque joints. But what really caught attention was its artificial skin, so sensitive it can sense a human approaching before contact happens. The robot played chess, danced, and held conversations without missing a beat. Powered by NVIDIA Isaac GR0000T and connected to anywhere averse for anyone can share learn skills across robot fleets. In a world heading toward [music] intelligence, this German humanoid might be the blueprint for safe social robots in healthcare and elder care. Not all innovation stayed on land. One of the most unexpected robots at CES came from under the sea. Beatbot's robo turtle, nicknamed Tertini, glided onto the show floor like a living creature. Modeled after a sea turtle, it swims, dives, and glides with astonishing grace. Solar panels keep it running for long missions, while its AI analyzes water quality and tracks marine life. This isn't a novelty. It's an eco robot built for environmental research and coral [music] reef preservation. Beatbot made one thing clear. Robots aren't just helping humans, they're helping the planet. Among all the advanced machines, one robot stood out for a completely different reason. It made people feel something. Tumbits Jenny shaped like a golden retriever puppy and developed with Jim Henson's Creature Shop, wagged her tail, blinked, [music] and tilted her head when spoken to. She responded to touch, voice, and tone with lifelike warmth. Designed to comfort seniors and [music] people with dementia, Jenny showed that soft robotics can ease loneliness. At $1,500, she's already entering care homes across the US, proving that empathy might be the most advanced technology of all. Practicality quietly stole the spotlight in the home robot category. Switchbots Honoro H1, long and strangely charming, winning Gadget's best of CES [music] award. It rolled around collecting laundry and loading washing machines with patient precision. Instead of relying on cloud servers, Honoro runs its AI locally using Omnisense vision language processing, allowing it to see, think, and act offline. In a show filled with dramatic humanoids, this simple machine proved that usefulness often beats spectacle. Healthcare and Assistive Robotics took another step

with patient precision. Instead of relying on cloud servers, Honoro runs its AI locally using Omnisense vision language processing, allowing it to see, think, and act offline. In a show filled with dramatic humanoids, this simple machine proved that usefulness often beats spectacle. Healthcare and Assistive Robotics took another step debut. The GR3, a softshelled humanoid, played chess, danced smoothly, and recognized faces and voices in real time. With 55 degrees of freedom and a focus on safety, it demonstrated how robots can work comfortably in hospitals and rehab centers without intimidation. Limx Dynamics showcased Tron 1 as a serious platform built around one core focus, mobility. Designed for advanced locomotion research, Tron 1 emphasized speed, balance, and energy efficiency rather than flashy demonstrations. Its movement reflected careful actuator tuning, stable gate control, and a clear push toward realworld bipedal performance. More importantly, Tron 1 represents a foundation, not a finished story. [music] With LIMX already signaling progress toward a next generation Tron 2, the platform is clearly evolving fast. Instead of chasing spectacle, Limx is refining the mechanics that will define how future humanoids walk, run, and operate efficiently in complex environments. Tron 1 focuses on locomotion research while robots like Atlas and Uni show full body performance. Which approach do you think actually matters more for the future of humanoids? Tell me below. I'm reading your replies. Dexterity took center stage when Sherpa from Singapore challenged humans to a live game of pingpong. The robot served, returned shots, and even lost politely. But the real story was its robotic hand capable of gripping, twisting, and adjusting with extraordinary precision. This level of control could one day be used in surgery, assembly, or advanced training. Smaller startups also brought big ideas. Alabot revealed early concepts of AI companions focused [music] on personal interaction rather than labor. While details were limited, the message was clear. Robots are increasingly designed to understand people, not just assist them. Problem solving took a playful turn when Clutterbot introduced [music] ROI, a tiny robot designed to clean up toys. Using computer vision and a dustpan style tray, ROI collects multiple items at once [music] and sorts them into bins. While others chased humanoid grandeur, Clutterbot solved one everyday problem perfectly. Personality came through clearly with Alex from D Robotics. This waistup robot posed for photos, formed heart shapes with its hands, and moved with expressive precision. Designed for manufacturing in service roles, Alex showed that mobility isn't required for presence. Two capable arms can still feel alive. Sharp offered a glimpse into personal AI with Poctomo, a tiny robot carried in a clear backpack. It records daily activity, recognizes faces, and provides [music] personal updates. Exclusive to Japan for now, Poctomo feels like the next evolution beyond smartphones. An AI that lives alongside you, not inside a screen. Cuteness had its moment, too. Bibbo, the Mooney type robot inspired by Asian character culture, drew massive crowds. With its round face [music] and

[music] personal updates. Exclusive to Japan for now, Poctomo feels like the next evolution beyond smartphones. An AI that lives alongside you, not inside a screen. Cuteness had its moment, too. Bibbo, the Mooney type robot inspired by Asian character culture, drew massive crowds. With its round face [music] and productivity. It was built for joy. Thousands lined up for photos, proving charm still matters. Xerof from China introduced two small companions, the M1 and W1. The M1 is a desk-sized humanoid priced at $2,400, while the W1 rolls on tank style treads for $5,000. They didn't perform complex tasks, but they succeeded at something just as important. They made people smile. [music] In Eureka Park, companionship took a softer form. Kakamo, a fuzzy duo from Leidden's AI, followed visitors autonomously like loyal pets. No arms, no legs, just gentle behavior and presence. They embodied a new philosophy. Robots that don't replace humans, but reinforce connection. After seeing all these robots from factories to homes to companionship, are you excited about the future or cautious about it? Comment your final takeaway below. I'll be reading and replying to as many as I can. And that was CES 2026, a show where robots finally felt ready to leave the lab and enter our lives. From industrial powerhouses to emotional companions, every machine sent the same message. AI is no longer trapped in screens. This year wasn't about concepts. It was about commitment. The future of robotics isn't approaching. It's already here. 2026 is the year AI robots stopped feeling like experiments. Figure 03 is now running at human jogging speed. Optimus Gen 3 feels closer than people expected. HMD01 proved robots can work full shifts in real factories. Kepler's robot is working where humans face danger, doing real welding work. Hey guys, Alfie here. Welcome back to AI Nexus. Figure just quietly raised the bar for humanoid robots, and most people almost missed it. A short video drops online, framed almost like a joke, calling it a new fitness program at Figure. At first glance, it feels playful and easy to scroll past. But if you actually watch closely, it becomes clear this is not marketing fluff. What the video really shows is figure 03 moving with purpose. The robot transitions from a steady stride into a controlled run, accelerating forward while maintaining balance and stability. This is not a cautious demonstration designed to avoid mistakes. Figure 03 moves confidently, adjusts direction smoothly, and keeps momentum without hesitation. That moment alone challenges a long-standing belief that humanoid robots are slow, fragile, and limited to careful movements. Figure03 reaches speeds close to a human jogging pace, around 6 and 1/2 mph. That is staggering for a full-size humanoid robot. Even more impressive is how the robot handles motion changes. It accelerates quickly, slows down sharply, and executes controlled turns, all without stumbling. This shows real progress in balance, gate control, and dynamic stability, areas where humanoid robots usually fail [music] first.

That is staggering for a full-size humanoid robot. Even more impressive is how the robot handles motion changes. It accelerates quickly, slows down sharply, and executes controlled turns, all without stumbling. This shows real progress in balance, gate control, and dynamic stability, areas where humanoid robots usually fail [music] first. behavior is not a scripted animation or a pre-programmed routine. Figure powers this movement using its new Helix AI [music] system. Helix learns directly from human videos and transfers that behavior to the robot without step-by-step programming. In simple terms, figure 03 watches how humans move and figures out how to reproduce that motion using a robotic body. This process known as zeroot humantoroot transfer is a major breakthrough. This matters because speed unlocks usefulness. A robot that can move quickly can operate in real factories, busy warehouses, and homes without slowing everything down. Figure designed figure 03 for generalpurpose work, including folding laundry, washing dishes, and assisting in commercial environments. Running shows the robot can keep up with humans instead of holding them back. The fitness program framing may sound playful, but the message is serious. Figure is training robots the way athletes train bodies, focusing on strength, balance, coordination, [music] and recovery. When a humanoid robot can run confidently, it signals readiness for far more demanding tasks ahead. [music] This is not a gimmick. It is a preview of robots that move with purpose, speed, and confidence. And once robots can run, the line between human pace and robot pace begins to disappear. A short AI generated video featuring Tesla's humanoid robot Optimus recently went viral and not because of flashy visuals or clever humor. The clip shows Optimus dancing alongside Elon Musk wearing matching outfits and moving with smooth, confident body language. On the surface, it looks playful. But the reaction it triggered online revealed something more important. People began questioning whether they were looking at a believable preview of Optimus' future capabilities. The caption reads, "Feeling optimistic about the future. It feels light-hearted, almost deliberately unserious. Yet, the discussion that followed told a different story. What pushed this clip into viral territory wasn't the dance itself. It was the opening frame. That first image looked real [music] enough that viewers immediately started asking the same question. Was this an early glimpse of Optimus version 3? That question spread faster than the video. Some called it clever marketing. Others wondered if it was an accidental reveal. Either way, the clip worked because it felt believable, and believability is where perception starts to change. This moment didn't appear out of nowhere. Over the past year, Tesla has steadily released footage of Optimus walking, maintaining balance, manipulating objects, and performing coordinated movements with growing confidence. Each update quietly reset expectations. So, when people see Optimus dancing, the brain doesn't reject it. It accepts it as plausible. The conversation escalated further when Simon reacted to early Optimist development with a statement that caught attention. According to Simon, Optimus 3

and performing coordinated movements with growing confidence. Each update quietly reset expectations. So, when people see Optimus dancing, the brain doesn't reject it. It accepts it as plausible. The conversation escalated further when Simon reacted to early Optimist development with a statement that caught attention. According to Simon, Optimus 3 eventually forget Tesla ever made cars. That isn't casual hype. It reflects how the robot's trajectory looks to those closely observing its development. [music] It reframes Optimus as a future core product rather than a side experiment. Consider the scale implied by that shift. Cars scale in millions. Humanoid robots scale in billions. One robot per household. One per worker. one per job people avoid. That is the future being suggested here. Tesla has clarified that Optimus 3 has not been publicly revealed and current visuals still show earlier versions of the robot, but that clarification only amplifies the moment. If previous versions already feel this capable, expectations for what comes next are accelerating quickly. This is no longer just about a viral clip. It's about perception crossing a threshold. Optimus is no longer discussed like a prototype. It's being treated like an inevitable product. [music] And once that mindset locks in, momentum follows. Instead of chasing attention with flashy demos or viral [music] clips, Seammens made a deliberate move into humanoid robotics by testing one where it actually matters, inside a working factory. This was not a showcase or a concept reveal. It was a realworld pilot designed to answer a practical question about the future of industrial automation. Seammens partnered with a young British startup called humanoid and deployed a robot named HM01 Alpha inside the Seaman's electronics factory in Heirlong. The objective was straightforward. Could a humanoid style robot operate reliably in industrial logistics without disrupting the human workers already on the factory floor? What makes this test interesting [music] is what Seammens did not prioritize. HMD01 Alpha does not move like a movie humanoid and it does not try to mimic human walking. Instead, the robot uses a wheeled base and focuses entirely on utility. Its task was simple but demanding. Autonomously pick storage totes from stacked shelves, transport them across the factory floor, and place them onto conveyors for human workers. The system ran without remote control or human intervention, repeating the cycle [music] until the storage stacks were empty. The real story, however, is in the performance data. During the twoe pilot, the robot moved roughly 60 totes per hour. It operated continuously for more than 30 minutes at a time with daily uptime exceeding 8 hours. Autonomous pick and place success stayed above 90%. for an early deployment. Those numbers are significant. This was not a lab environment. It was a live electronics factory. Seammens described this project as a conservative first [music] step, and that choice was intentional. HMD01 Alpha uses simple industrial grippers rather than human-like hands. The design feels closer to a collaborative robot than a

numbers are significant. This was not a lab environment. It was a live electronics factory. Seammens described this project as a conservative first [music] step, and that choice was intentional. HMD01 Alpha uses simple industrial grippers rather than human-like hands. The design feels closer to a collaborative robot than a clearly prioritizing reliability and consistency over realism. a strategy that could shape which companies succeed first in industrial humanoids. Physically, the robot stands about 175 cm tall, weighs around 70 kg, and handles payloads of up to 15 kg. Humanoid also revealed that a bipeedal version of the robot learned stable walking within 48 hours using simulationbased training, offering a glimpse of what may follow. For now, Seammens demonstrated something important. Humanoid robots do not need to look human to deliver value. In industrial settings, usefulness comes from reliability. And in this pilot, the robot delivered exactly that. Highaltitude welding is one of the most dangerous jobs in industrial work. And that is exactly where Shanghai Kepler's humanoid robot K2 steps in. This robot is not doing a lab demo. This robot is operating in real conditions at height with real welding tools, replacing tasks that normally put human lives at risk. And here's the crazy part. It [music] is not fully autonomous. A human operator stays safely on the ground [music] wearing a VR headset and controls the K2 in real time, turning the robot into a physical avatar in dangerous environments. The Kepler [music] K2, also known as Bumblebee, is built specifically for dirty, dull, and dangerous work. The robot has 52 degrees of freedom, which means it can copy complex human arm, wrist, and body movements with shocking accuracy. That level of motion control is critical for welding, where even small angle errors can ruin a joint. The robot uses a hybrid actuator system that combines linear and rotary actuators, giving it both strength and precision. The result is a robot that can carry up to 30 kg while maintaining millimeter level accuracy. But here's the mind-blowing part. The K2 is not just strong and flexible. It is practical. The robot runs up to 8 hours on a single charge after just 1 hour of charging. That makes it suitable for full industrial shifts, not short demos. The layered VLA plus system helps the robot interpret commands and [music] stay stable in complex environments, while the human operator provides judgment and problem solving during tricky welds. This approach solves three problems at once. Safety improves because humans are removed from high altitude risk. Precision improves because a human brain still guides the task. And cost [music] drops because the K2 is priced around $35,000, making advanced humanoid labor accessible to real factories. This is not a future promise. This is a working robot quietly redefining industrial labor right
